steps.title: Calculate volume at STP
steps.content:
We use the combined gas law to find what volume this gas would occupy at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP), where $P_2 = 100,000\text{ Pa}$ and $T_2 = 273.15\text{ K}$.

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} \implies V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1 T_2}{P_2 T_1}$$

Substituting the values:
$$V_2 = \frac{99992\text{ Pa} \times 0.843\text{ dm}^3 \times 273.15\text{ K}}{100000\text{ Pa} \times 296.15\text{ K}} \approx 0.7775\text{ dm}^3$$

calculator.description: Calculate the volume at STP (V2).

steps.title: Determine amount of moles
steps.content:
Now, we use the **Molar volume at STP** constant from the data booklet to find the number of moles ($n$).

$$n = \frac{V_{\text{STP}}}{V_{\text{m}}} = \frac{0.7775\text{ dm}^3}{22.7\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.03425\text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Using the previous answer (Ans), enter Ans / 22.7 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Using the previous answer (Ans), enter Ans / 22.7 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Divide the previous result by 22.7.

calculator.description: Divide the volume at STP by the molar volume constant.

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) using the mass of the gas provided ($1.37\text{ g}$).

From the data booklet:
$$n = \frac{m}{M} \implies M = \frac{m}{n}$$

$$M = \frac{1.37\text{ g}}{0.03425\text{ mol}} = 40.0\text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1.37 / Ans and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type 1.37 divided by the previous result.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass.

parts.content: Once the experiment was over, the student was informed that the unknown gas was carbon dioxide, $\text{CO}_2$. Determine the percentage error in the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** values into the percentage error formula: $\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|44.01 - 40.0|}{44.01} \times 100$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the final result: $9.11\% \text{ (accept 9.1\% or 9\%)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate theoretical molar mass
steps.content:
To find the percentage error, we first need the **theoretical (accepted) value** for the molar mass of carbon dioxide ($M_{\text{CO}_2}$). 

Using the periodic table values:
- Carbon ($C$): $12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- Oxygen ($O$): $16.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$M_{\text{theoretical}} = 12.01 + (2 \times 16.00) = 44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of CO2.

steps.title: Apply percentage error formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standard formula for percentage error. This compares our experimental result ($40.0\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) to the theoretical value we just calculated:

$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|\text{theoretical} - \text{experimental}|}{\text{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:
$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|44.01 - 40.0|}{44.01} \times 100\%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage error value.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us approximately $9.1115...\%$. 

Rounding to an appropriate number of significant figures (usually matching the experimental data), we get:
$$\text{Percentage error} = 9.11\%$$

| 20 | 108 |
| 40 | 116 |

steps.title: Convert temperatures to Kelvin
steps.content:
To verify Charles's Law, we must use the absolute temperature scale. We convert Celsius ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) to Kelvin (\text{K}) using the relationship:

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273$$

This conversion is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**. For each data point, we add 273 to the Celsius value.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, perform the addition for each value.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0+273, 20+273, etc. on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Create a list and add 273.

calculator.description: Convert all Celsius values to Kelvin by adding 273.

highlights.text: Convert the temperatures from $^{\circ}\text{C}$ to Kelvin

steps.title: Organize data for plotting
steps.content:
Now we can list the new coordinates $(T, V)$ for our graph. The question specifies that temperature ($T$) should be on the $x$-axis and volume ($V$) on the $y$-axis.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline T \text{ (K)} & V \text{ (cm}^3\text{)} \\ \hline 273 & 100 \\ 293 & 108 \\ 313 & 116 \\ 333 & 124 \\ 353 & 132 \\ 373 & 140 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

steps.title: Label axes and plot
steps.content:
When plotting the graph, it is essential to label your axes correctly to earn full marks. 

1.  **$x$-axis**: Label as **$T$ (K)** or **Temperature (K)**.
2.  **$y$-axis**: Label as **$V$ (cm$^3$)** or **Volume (cm$^3$)**.

Carefully mark each point from the table on the provided grid.

highlights.text: Label axes with appropriate units: $V$ ($\text{cm}^3$) and $T$ ($\text{K}$)

steps.title: Draw the best-fit line
steps.content:
Finally, draw a **single best-fit straight line** through the points. Use a ruler to ensure it is perfectly straight. 

Do not connect the dots in a 'zig-zag' fashion; instead, draw one line that represents the overall trend. Since the data is perfectly linear in this example, the line should pass directly through all points.

highlights.text: Draw a single best-fit straight line through the data points

parts.content: Determine the relationship: Based on your graph, determine if there is a linear relationship between $V$ and $T$. Calculate the slope of the line and discuss whether the results confirm that volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature as predicted by Charles's Law.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the plot shows a straight line, which confirms a linear relationship between $V$ and $T$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the slope using two points from the line (e.g., $(273, 100)$ and $(373, 140)$): 
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{140 - 100}{373 - 273} = 0.4~\text{cm}^3\text{/K}$$ <marks>M1A1</marks>
- Conclude that while the relationship is linear, the data does not confirm direct proportionality ($V \propto T$) because the $y$-intercept is not zero (at $T = 0~\text{K}$, $V \approx -9.2~\text{cm}^3$), suggesting potential systematic error or non-ideal behavior <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Convert Temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
To analyze Charles's Law ($V \propto T$), we must work with the absolute temperature scale (Kelvin). We convert the Celsius temperatures using the relationship $T(K) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273$.

Comparing the endpoints of our data:
- At $0^\circ\text{C}$: $T = 0 + 273 = 273\text{ K}$
- At $100^\circ\text{C}$: $T = 100 + 273 = 373\text{ K}$

steps.title: Identify the Linear Relationship
steps.content:
A linear relationship exists if the change in volume is proportional to the change in temperature. In the data table, every increase of $20^\circ\text{C}$ (or $20\text{ K}$) results in a constant increase of $8\text{ cm}^3$ in volume. 

Since the rate of change is constant, the relationship between $V$ and $T$ is **linear**, forming a straight line on a graph. This earns you the first mark.

highlights.text: State that the plot shows a straight line, which confirms a linear relationship between V and T

steps.title: Calculate the Gradient
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the slope (gradient) of the line using the formula $m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$. We will use our converted Kelvin temperatures for the x-axis.

Using the points $(273\text{ K}, 100\text{ cm}^3)$ and $(373\text{ K}, 140\text{ cm}^3)$:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{140 - 100}{373 - 273} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4\text{ cm}^3/\text{K}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division: 40 divided by 100.

desmos.instructions: Type the gradient formula into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient (slope) of the line.

highlights.text: Calculate the slope using two points from the line

steps.title: Determine the Y-Intercept
steps.content:
To check for direct proportionality, we need to find the volume at absolute zero ($T = 0\text{ K}$). We use the equation of a straight line, $V = mT + c$.

Substituting $V = 100$, $m = 0.4$, and $T = 273$:

$$\begin{aligned} 100 &= 0.4(273) + c \\ 100 &= 109.2 + c \\ c &= 100 - 109.2 \\ c &= -9.2\text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

The $y$-intercept is $-9.2\text{ cm}^3$.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the product of the slope and temperature from the volume: 100 - (0.4 * 273).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the intercept using the linear equation.

calculator.description: Calculate the y-intercept of the V-T graph.

steps.title: Evaluate Direct Proportionality
steps.content:
For a relationship to be **directly proportional** ($V \propto T$), the line must pass through the origin $(0,0)$. This means the $y$-intercept must be zero.

Because our calculated intercept is $-9.2\text{ cm}^3$ (not zero), the data does **not** confirm direct proportionality. This discrepancy might be due to systematic errors in the experiment or the gas deviating from ideal behavior at lower temperatures.

In the IB, you would refer to the ideal gas law to explain that while the theory predicts $V \propto T$, real-world data often shows small deviations.

highlights.text: Conclude that while the relationship is linear, the data does not confirm direct proportionality (V ∝ T) because the y-intercept is not zero

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
First, we look at the data to see if the relationship is linear. A linear relationship means that for a constant change in temperature, there is a constant change in volume. 

Looking at the table, for every $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ increase in temperature, the volume increases by exactly $8\text{ cm}^3$ (e.g., $108 - 100 = 8$ and $116 - 108 = 8$). Since the rate of change is constant, we can conclude that the plot shows a straight line, confirming a linear relationship.

highlights.text: determine if there is a linear relationship between $V$ and $T$

steps.title: Calculate the slope
steps.content:
To calculate the slope, we use two points from the data. Since Charles's Law relates volume to absolute temperature ($T$ in Kelvin), we first convert the temperatures using $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273$.

Using the first point $(273\text{ K}, 100\text{ cm}^3)$ and the last point $(373\text{ K}, 140\text{ cm}^3)$:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} = \frac{140 - 100}{373 - 273}$$

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4\text{ cm}^3/\text{K}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division of the change in volume by the change in temperature.

desmos.instructions: Type the fraction expression to find the slope value.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope using two data points in Kelvin.

highlights.text: Calculate the slope of the line

steps.title: Perform V/T ratio analysis
steps.content:
Charles's Law states that for an ideal gas, $V \propto T$. Mathematically, this means the ratio $\frac{V}{T}$ must be constant for all data points when $T$ is in Kelvin.

Let's test two points from our data:
1. At $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($273\text{ K}$): $\frac{V}{T} = \frac{100}{273} \approx 0.366\text{ cm}^3/\text{K}$
2. At $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($373\text{ K}$): $\frac{V}{T} = \frac{140}{373} \approx 0.375\text{ cm}^3/\text{K}$

Because these ratios are not equal, the volume is not strictly constant over the temperature range in terms of a simple ratio.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the V/T ratios for the first and last points.

desmos.instructions: Compare the decimal values of the two ratios.

calculator.description: Calculate the V/T ratio for two different points to check for constancy.

steps.title: Discuss direct proportionality
steps.content:
For a relationship to be **directly proportional**, the line must be linear AND pass through the origin $(0,0)$. Since our $\frac{V}{T}$ ratios are not constant, the $y$-intercept is not zero (calculating it would give $V \approx -9.2\text{ cm}^3$ at $0\text{ K}$).

Therefore, while the relationship is linear, the results **do not confirm** direct proportionality as predicted by Charles's Law. This discrepancy suggests potential systematic error in the experiment or that the gas is not behaving ideally.

highlights.text: discuss whether the results confirm that volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
First, we observe the data to determine the nature of the relationship. Looking at the table, for every $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ increase in temperature, the volume increases by exactly $8\text{ cm}^3$ ($108-100=8$, $116-108=8$, etc.). 

Because the change in volume is constant relative to the change in temperature, we can conclude that the relationship between $V$ and $T$ is **linear**. On a graph, this would appear as a straight line.

highlights.text: the plot shows a straight line, which confirms a linear relationship

steps.title: Calculate the slope
steps.content:
To find the slope ($m$) of this linear relationship, we use the formula for the gradient between two points $(T_1, V_1)$ and $(T_2, V_2)$:

$$m = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

Using the points $(0, 100)$ and $(100, 140)$ from the table:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{140 - 100}{100 - 0} \\ m &= \frac{40}{100} \\ m &= 0.4 \text{ cm}^3/^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

Note that since a change of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\text{ K}$, the slope is also $0.4\text{ cm}^3/\text{K}$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the fraction (140 - 100) / (100 - 0).

desmos.instructions: Type the slope formula directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope using the first and last data points.

steps.title: Determine the x-intercept
steps.content:
Charles's Law states that volume is directly proportional to **absolute** temperature ($V \propto T_{K}$). For this to be true, the volume must be zero when the temperature is absolute zero ($-273.15^{\circ}\text{C}$).

Using our linear equation $V = mT + c$, where the y-intercept $c = 100$ (since $V=100$ when $T=0$):

$$V = 0.4T + 100$$

To find the x-intercept (the temperature where volume would theoretically be zero), we set $V = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= 0.4T + 100 \\ -100 &= 0.4T \\ T &= \frac{-100}{0.4} \\ T &= -250^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Solve the equation 0 = 0.4x + 100 or calculate -100 / 0.4.

desmos.instructions: Plot the line y = 0.4x + 100 and identify the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Calculate the x-intercept by dividing the negative y-intercept by the slope.

steps.title: Evaluate direct proportionality
steps.content:
Finally, we compare our extrapolated value to the theoretical value for absolute zero. 

Ideal gas behavior predicts the x-intercept should be $-273.15^{\circ}\text{C}$. Our calculated intercept is $-250^{\circ}\text{C}$. 

Because the intercept is not exactly absolute zero, the data shows a linear relationship but **does not perfectly confirm direct proportionality** to absolute temperature. This discrepancy could be due to systematic errors in the experiment or the gas not behaving exactly like an ideal gas at these conditions.

highlights.text: the data does not confirm direct proportionality ($V \propto T$) because the $y$-intercept is not zero

parts.content: Assume the theoretical volume at $273~\text{K}$ is $102~\text{cm}^3$. Calculate the percentage error of the experimental value at $273~\text{K}$ obtained from your graph. If each volume measurement has an uncertainty of $\pm 2~\text{cm}^3$, discuss whether the experimental result is consistent with the theoretical value.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the experimental volume at $273~\text{K}$ from the graph/table as $100~\text{cm}^3$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the percentage error:
$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|102 - 100|}{102} \times 100 \approx 1.96\% \approx 2.0\%$$ <marks>M1A1</marks>
- Conclude that the result is consistent with the theoretical value because the percentage error ($≈ 2\%$) is within the relative uncertainty of the volume measurements ($\frac{2}{100} = 2\%$) <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify the experimental value
steps.content:
First, we need to find the experimental volume at $273\text{ K}$. Since $273\text{ K}$ is equivalent to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, we look at the provided data table.

At $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, the recorded experimental volume is $100\text{ cm}^3$.

steps.title: Calculate the percentage error
steps.content:
To find the percentage error, we compare the difference between the theoretical value ($102\text{ cm}^3$) and the experimental value ($100\text{ cm}^3$) to the theoretical value:

$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|\text{theoretical} - \text{experimental}|}{\text{theoretical}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|102 - 100|}{102} \times 100 \approx 1.96\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the absolute difference divided by 102, then multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Type the percentage error formula into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage error based on theoretical and experimental values.

highlights.text: theoretical volume at 273 K is 102 cm3

steps.title: Calculate the percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the relative (percentage) uncertainty of the volume measurement. The question states the absolute uncertainty is $\pm 2\text{ cm}^3$ for a measurement of $100\text{ cm}^3$:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \frac{\text{absolute uncertainty}}{\text{measured value}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \frac{2}{100} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the absolute uncertainty by the volume, then multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Divide the uncertainty by the measurement and multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the relative uncertainty of the volume measurement.

steps.title: Determine consistency
steps.content:
In science, an experimental result is considered **consistent** with a theoretical value if the percentage error is less than or equal to the percentage uncertainty.

Comparing our values:
- **Percentage Error:** $\approx 1.96\%$
- **Percentage Uncertainty:** $2.0\%$

Since $1.96\% < 2.0\%$, the experimental result is **consistent** with the theoretical value. The difference can be explained by the random uncertainties in the measurements.

steps.title: Identify experimental volume
steps.content:
To find the experimental volume at $273\text{ K}$, we first convert the temperature to Celsius. Since $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$, $273\text{ K}$ is approximately $0^{\circ}\text{C}$. 

Looking at the data table provided in the question, at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, the experimental volume is $100\text{ cm}^3$.

steps.title: Calculate percentage error
steps.content:
The percentage error is calculated by comparing the difference between the experimental and theoretical values to the theoretical value:

$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|\text{Theoretical} - \text{Experimental}|}{\text{Theoretical}} \times 100$$

Substituting the values:
$$\text{Percentage error} = \frac{|102 - 100|}{102} \times 100 \approx 1.96\%$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (abs(102 - 100) / 102) * 100 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (abs(102 - 100) / 102) * 100 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage error for the volume measurement.

highlights.text: theoretical volume at 273 K is 102 cm3

steps.title: Determine absolute uncertainty range
steps.content:
The absolute uncertainty range is defined as the experimental value plus or minus the given uncertainty ($V \pm \Delta V$). 

Given the uncertainty is $\pm 2\text{ cm}^3$:
$$\text{Range} = 100 \pm 2\text{ cm}^3 = [98\text{ cm}^3, 102\text{ cm}^3]$$

steps.title: Assess consistency
steps.content:
A result is considered consistent with the theoretical value if the theoretical value falls within the range of experimental uncertainty.

Since the theoretical value of $102\text{ cm}^3$ falls exactly at the upper bound of the experimental range ($98\text{ cm}^3$ to $102\text{ cm}^3$), the experimental result is **consistent** with the theoretical value.

This demonstrates that the small difference ($1.96\%$) can be explained by the random errors accounted for in the volume measurements.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.5.2 Deviations from ideal gas behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Both parts ask for reasons gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure and low temperature, which is directly the content of deviations from ideal gas behavior (finite molecular volume and intermolecular attractions becoming significant).

parts.content: Explain why gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high pressures.
parts.markscheme:
- At high pressures, gas particles are closer together <marks>1</marks>
- Volume of particles becomes significant compared to container volume <marks>1</marks>
- This violates ideal gas assumption of negligible particle volume <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the ideal gas assumption
steps.content: To understand why gases deviate from ideal behavior, we first need to look at **Topic S1.5: Ideal gases**. The Kinetic Molecular Theory (KMT) assumes that gas particles are so small that their own volume is negligible (essentially zero) compared to the total volume of the container.

steps.title: Effect of high pressure
steps.content: When a gas is at high pressure, the particles are forced much closer together. This means there is far less empty space between them than there is at lower pressures.

steps.title: Significant particle volume
steps.content: Using the **Particle Volume Approach**, we see that at these high pressures, the actual volume occupied by the gas particles themselves becomes a significant fraction of the total container volume. We can no longer ignore the space the particles take up.

steps.title: Deviation from the model
steps.content:
Because the particles take up real space, the "free space" available for them to move is smaller than the total volume ($V$) used in the ideal gas equation:

$$PV = nRT$$

This violation of the "negligible volume" assumption is why real gases do not follow the ideal gas law perfectly at high pressures.

steps.title: Recall ideal gas assumptions
steps.content:
To understand why gases deviate from ideal behavior, we first look at the **Kinetic Molecular Theory (KMT)**. For an 'ideal' gas, we assume two main things: 
1. There are **no intermolecular forces** (attractions or repulsions) between particles.
2. The particles themselves have **negligible volume** (they are treated as point masses).

These assumptions allow us to use the ideal gas equation found in your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

steps.title: Particles at high pressure
steps.content:
When a gas is at very high pressure, the particles are forced into a much smaller space. Because the space is restricted, the distance between the individual gas particles decreases significantly.

According to the markscheme, the first key observation is that at high pressures, gas particles are closer together.

highlights.text: At high pressures, gas particles are closer together

steps.title: Impact of intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Using the **Intermolecular Forces Approach**, we see that as particles get closer together, the attractive forces between them are no longer negligible. 

These attractions pull the particles toward each other, which means they strike the walls of the container with less force than they would if no attractions existed. This causes the measured pressure of a real gas to be lower than the pressure predicted by the ideal gas law.

steps.title: Significant particle volume
steps.content:
As the particles are packed tighter, the physical space (volume) occupied by the gas particles themselves becomes a significant fraction of the total container volume. 

In an ideal gas, we assume particles take up zero space, but in a real gas at high pressure, this is no longer true. The volume of particles becomes significant compared to container volume.

highlights.text: Volume of particles becomes significant compared to container volume

steps.title: Conclusion and marks
steps.content:
Because the particles now occupy a non-negligible amount of space and exert attractive forces on each other, the fundamental assumptions of the ideal gas model are broken. 

To secure full marks, ensure you state that this violates ideal gas assumption of negligible particle volume.

highlights.text: This violates ideal gas assumption of negligible particle volume

parts.content: Explain why gases deviate from ideal behaviour at low temperatures.
parts.markscheme:
- At low temperatures, gas particles have lower kinetic energy <marks>1</marks>
- Intermolecular forces become more significant <marks>1</marks>
- This causes deviations from ideal behaviour as particles attract each other <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content:
According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory (KMT)**, the absolute temperature of a gas is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of its particles. As the temperature decreases, the particles move much more slowly.

This relates to **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: At low temperatures, gas particles have lower kinetic energy

steps.title: Analyze intermolecular forces
steps.content:
In an ideal gas model, we assume there are no attractive forces between particles. However, in real gases, **intermolecular forces (IMFs)** exist. 

When particles move slowly (at low temperatures), they spend more time near each other. This allows the attractive forces to become significant enough to influence their motion, pulling particles toward one another.

steps.title: Explain the deviation
steps.content: Because the particles attract each other, they do not collide with the container walls as often or with as much force as predicted by the ideal gas law ($PV=nRT$). This attraction means the gas no longer behaves like a collection of independent particles, leading to a deviation from ideal behavior.

steps.title: Identify the relevant postulate
steps.content:
To understand why gases deviate from ideal behavior, we first look at the **Ideal Gas Model** (Topic S1.5.1). One of its key postulates is that there are **no intermolecular forces of attraction** between gas particles. This assumption allows us to use the ideal gas equation:

$$PV = nRT$$

steps.title: Link temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content: As explained in **Topic S1.1.3**, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. When we reach **low temperatures**, the particles move much more slowly because they have significantly lower kinetic energy.

steps.title: Analyze the impact of attractions
steps.content:
In an ideal gas, particles would just zoom past each other. However, in reality, particles have intermolecular forces (like London dispersion forces). At low temperatures, because the particles are moving so slowly, these **intermolecular forces become significant**. 

Instead of bouncing off each other or the container walls perfectly, the particles begin to attract one another, which "pulls" them together and reduces the force of their collisions with the walls.

steps.title: Explain the deviation
steps.content:
Because the particles are attracting each other, the gas no longer behaves like the "ideal" model where particles are independent. This attraction causes the actual pressure and volume to differ from the values predicted by the ideal gas law, leading to **deviation from ideal behaviour**.

To summarize for your marks:
1. At low temperatures, particles have **lower kinetic energy** (1 mark).
2. **Intermolecular forces become significant** (or particles attract each other), failing the ideal gas postulate (1 mark).

An industrial gas mixture contains helium and argon. The mixture is stored in a container, and data on the volume, pressure, and temperature of the gas mixture is collected under different conditions.

\hline \text { A } & 0 & 101.3 & 22.4 & 1.00 \\

\hline \text { C } & 50 & ? & 22.4 & 2.00 \\

\hline \text { E } & 150 & 202.6 & ? & 1.50 \\

parts.content: Deduce the missing value for the pressure at 50 °C (Source C).
parts.markscheme:
- Convert temperature to Kelvin: $T = 50 + 273 = 323\text{ K}$
- Substitute correct values into the ideal gas equation: $P = \frac{2.00 \times 8.31 \times 323}{22.4}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final pressure: $P = 240\text{ kPa}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify Source C variables
steps.content:
To solve for the missing pressure, we first need to extract the known values for **Source C** from the table:

*   Temperature ($T$) = $50 ^\circ\text{C}$
*   Volume ($V$) = $22.4 \text{ dm}^3$
*   Number of moles ($n$) = $2.00 \text{ mol}$

This falls under **Topic S1.5: Ideal gases** in your syllabus. We will use the Ideal Gas Equation to find the missing pressure ($P$).

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
In all gas law calculations, temperature must be in Kelvin ($K$). We convert from Celsius by adding $273$.

$$T = 50 + 273 = 323 \text{ K}$$

steps.title: Apply Ideal Gas Equation
steps.content:
We use the Ideal Gas Equation found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

To find pressure, we rearrange the formula to solve for $P$:

$$P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

We also need the gas constant ($R$) from the **Physical Constants** section:

$$R = 8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final pressure
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into our rearranged equation. 

Note: In the IB curriculum, when using $P$ in $\text{kPa}$ and $V$ in $\text{dm}^3$, the units are consistent with $R = 8.31$ because $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{dm}^3 = 1 \text{ Joule}$.

$$\begin{aligned} P &= \frac{2.00 \text{ mol} \times 8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1} \times 323 \text{ K}}{22.4 \text{ dm}^3} \\ P &= 239.65... \text{ kPa} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to the appropriate number of significant figures (usually 3 based on the data provided), we get **$240 \text{ kPa}$**.

gdc.instructions: Enter (2.00 * 8.31 * 323) / 22.4 and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type (2.00 * 8.31 * 323) / 22.4 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the pressure using the ideal gas equation values.

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
In gas law calculations, we must always use the absolute temperature scale (Kelvin). We convert Celsius to Kelvin by adding 273.

For **Source A**:
$$T_A = 0 + 273 = 273\text{ K}$$

For **Source C**:
$$T_C = 50 + 273 = 323\text{ K}$$

steps.title: Set up the comparison
steps.content:
The Ideal Gas Law is given by:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since $R$ is a constant, we can rearrange this to show that for any state:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{n_2 T_2}$$

We will compare **Source C** (our unknown) to **Source A** (our reference).

steps.title: Simplify and substitute values
steps.content:
Looking at the data table, we notice that the volume for both Source A and Source C is the same ($22.4\text{ dm}^3$). Because $V_A = V_C$, the volume terms cancel out of our equation:

$$\frac{P_A}{n_A T_A} = \frac{P_C}{n_C T_C}$$

Now, substitute the known values into the equation to solve for $P_C$:

$$\frac{101.3}{1.00 \times 273} = \frac{P_C}{2.00 \times 323}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final pressure
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate $P_C$:

$$P_C = \frac{101.3 \times 2.00 \times 323}{1.00 \times 273}$$

Using a calculator, we find the pressure for Source C. We round the result to three significant figures as per the provided data.

$$P_C \approx 240\text{ kPa}$$

gdc.instructions: In the calculate app, type (101.3 * 2 * 323) / (1 * 273) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final pressure using the combined gas law ratio.

parts.content: Explain why the number of moles changes for Source D compared to Source B and C.

1 mark total

steps.title: Define the physical meaning
steps.content: In chemistry, the **number of moles** ($n$) represents the physical amount of substance present in a sample. Conceptually, if you have a gas in a container, the moles tell you exactly how many gas particles (atoms of helium and argon in this case) are inside.

steps.title: Analyze the change
steps.content:
For Sources B and C, the amount of gas remains constant at $2.00$ mol. However, if we look at the conditions for Source D (higher pressure and volume compared to temperature), the ideal gas law indicates a different value for $n$.

Using the ideal gas equation from your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Solving for $n$:

$$n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

If the result for $n$ is different from $2.00$ mol, it means the total number of gas particles inside the container has changed.

gdc.instructions: Enter (405.2 * 24.5) / (8.31 * (100 + 273.15)) into the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Calculate (405.2 * 24.5) / (8.31 * (100 + 273.15)). Note: Pressure is in kPa and Volume in dm^3, which cancels out the units correctly for n.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the moles for Source D.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles (n) for Source D to verify it has changed from 2.00 mol.

steps.title: Determine physical cause
steps.content: Physically, the only way for the number of moles ($n$) to change is if the system is **not closed**. This implies that gas has been physically **added to or removed from** the container (for example, through a deliberate modification or an accidental leak).

highlights.text: State that gas has been added to or removed from the container (e.g., due to a leak or deliberate modification).

steps.title: Identify the formula and variables
steps.content:
To determine why the number of moles changes, we first need to calculate the actual number of moles ($n$) present in **Source D** using the ideal gas law from your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

From the table for **Source D**, we have:
- Pressure ($P$) = $405.2 \text{ kPa}$
- Volume ($V$) = $24.5 \text{ dm}^3$
- Temperature ($T$) = $100 \, ^\circ\text{C}$

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
Before using the ideal gas equation, we must convert the temperature from Celsius to Kelvin. In the IB curriculum, we add $273$ (or $273.15$ for precision) to the Celsius value:

$$T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \text{ K}$$

steps.title: Calculate moles for Source D
steps.content:
Now, rearrange the formula to solve for $n$ and substitute the values. We use the gas constant $R = 8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$ from the data booklet. Note that $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{dm}^3 = 1 \text{ J}$, so the units cancel out correctly.

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{PV}{RT} \\ n &= \frac{405.2 \times 24.5}{8.31 \times 373.15} \\ n &\approx 3.20 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Source D contains approximately $3.20 \text{ moles}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter (405.2 * 24.5) / (8.31 * 373.15) into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to calculate n.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles for Source D.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
By comparing the calculated value for **Source D** ($3.20 \text{ mol}$) with the values given for **Source B** and **Source C** ($2.00 \text{ mol}$), we see a significant increase.

Since the number of moles represents the amount of matter, this change indicates that **gas has been added to the container**.

parts.content: Researchers studying the relationship between temperature and volume at constant pressure compile data from different sources but note inconsistencies in the values recorded. Suggest one problem when comparing the data from these sources and one suggestion to improve the accuracy of the comparison.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify a problem: Experimental setups may vary between sources, such as differences in container materials, measurement device precision, or gas composition impurities. <marks>R1</marks>
- Suggest an improvement: Use identical apparatus and ensure consistent initial gas mixtures for all experimental runs. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the source of inconsistencies
steps.content: When data is compiled from **different sources** (A through E), we must consider that each source likely used a unique experimental setup. In the context of gas laws, small differences in how measurements are taken can lead to systematic errors that make the results difficult to compare directly.

highlights.text: compile data from different sources but note inconsistencies in the values recorded

steps.title: Identify the procedural problem
steps.content:
A major problem is the lack of **procedural consistency**. Different sources might use containers made of different materials (which expand differently with temperature), or they might have used gas mixtures with varying levels of impurities. 

For example, if Source A used $99.9\%$ pure Helium but Source B used $95\%$ pure Helium, their observed volumes ($V$) at a specific temperature ($T$) would not follow the exact same trend due to the different behavior of the impurities.

steps.title: Suggest a procedural improvement
steps.content:
To improve accuracy, researchers should standardize the experiment. This means using **identical apparatus** (same container type and same sensors) and ensuring a **consistent gas composition** (using the same batch/purity of gas) across all trials.

By keeping the equipment and materials the same, any systematic error remains constant, allowing for a much more reliable comparison of the relationship between temperature and volume.

steps.title: Finalize the 2-mark answer
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, here is how you should structure your final response:

*   **Problem:** The experimental setups may vary between sources, leading to differences in container expansion or variations in gas purity levels.
*   **Improvement:** Use the same apparatus for all measurements and ensure the initial gas mixture is identical in composition and purity for every run.

steps.title: Define the relationship
steps.content: The researchers are investigating the relationship between temperature ($T$) and volume ($V$). According to Charles's Law (part of the ideal gas model in **Topic S1.5**), the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature, but this only holds true if the pressure ($P$) and the amount of gas ($n$) remain constant.

highlights.text: relationship between temperature and volume at constant pressure

steps.title: Identify uncontrolled variables
steps.content:
Using a **Variable Control Approach**, we look at the table to see if other factors were kept the same. 

Notice that between Source A and Source B, not only does the temperature change, but the pressure and number of moles also change:
- Source A: $$P = 101.3\text{ kPa}, n = 1.00\text{ mol}$$
- Source B: $$P = 202.6\text{ kPa}, n = 2.00\text{ mol}$$

If we compare the volumes from these different sources, we cannot tell if the change in volume was caused by the temperature change or by the changes in pressure and moles. This is a significant problem for a valid scientific comparison.

steps.title: Identify experimental inconsistencies
steps.content:
Another problem when compiling data from "different sources" is that each lab might use different equipment. 

Variations in the precision of measurement devices, the material of the container (which might expand differently), or impurities in the gas mixture can lead to inconsistent results that don't match the ideal gas law perfectly.

steps.title: Suggest improvements
steps.content:
To improve the accuracy of the comparison, the researchers should standardize the experiment. This involves:
1. **Controlling variables**: Ensure the pressure ($P$) and the number of moles ($n$) are identical for every data point.
2. **Standardizing apparatus**: Use the same measuring instruments and containers to eliminate systematic errors between different sources.

**Awarding marks:**
- 1 mark for identifying the problem (e.g., uncontrolled variables or varying experimental setups).
- 1 mark for suggesting a valid improvement (e.g., using identical apparatus or ensuring consistent gas mixtures).

steps.title: Analyze the data sources
steps.content: The question notes that researchers found **inconsistencies** when comparing data from different sources (A through E). When data is compiled from multiple independent researchers, we must consider that each source likely used different experimental setups and measuring instruments.

highlights.text: note inconsistencies in the values recorded

steps.title: Identify the measurement problem
steps.content:
A major problem when comparing these sources is the variation in the **precision and calibration** of the instruments used (such as thermometers, gas syringes, and pressure gauges). 

For example, Source A might have used a digital pressure gauge with an uncertainty of $\pm 0.1\text{ kPa}$, while Source B used an analog gauge with an uncertainty of $\pm 1.0\text{ kPa}$. If these instruments were not calibrated against the same standard, systematic errors would be introduced, making it difficult to compare the values accurately.

steps.title: Suggest an improvement
steps.content: To improve the accuracy of the comparison, the researchers should **standardize the experimental protocol**. This includes using identical apparatus across all trials and ensuring that all measuring devices are calibrated using the same reference standard. This reduces the variability in experimental uncertainty between the different sources.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme requirements:

*   **Problem:** Differences in measurement device precision, calibration, or experimental setups between sources.
*   **Improvement:** Use identical apparatus and ensure consistent initial conditions (like gas purity) for all runs.

parts.content: Calculate the missing value for the volume at 150 °C (Source E).
parts.markscheme:
- Convert temperature to Kelvin: $T = 150 + 273 = 423\text{ K}$
- Substitute correct values into the ideal gas equation: $V = \frac{1.50 \times 8.31 \times 423}{202.6}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final volume: $V = 26.0\text{ dm}^3$ ($0.0260\text{ m}^3$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
To find the volume for Source E, we first extract the relevant data from the table:

*   **Temperature ($T$):** $150 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
*   **Pressure ($P$):** $202.6 \, \text{kPa}$
*   **Number of moles ($n$):** $1.50 \, \text{mol}$

We also need the ideal gas constant ($R$) from the data booklet.

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
In the ideal gas equation, temperature must always be in Kelvin ($K$). We convert the Celsius temperature by adding $273$:

$$T = 150 + 273 = 423 \, \text{K}$$

steps.title: Rearrange Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
The Ideal Gas Law is found in your data booklet. We need to solve for volume ($V$), so we rearrange the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= nRT \\ V &= \frac{nRT}{P} \end{aligned}$$

Note: When using $P$ in $\text{kPa}$ and $R = 8.31$, the resulting volume will be in $\text{dm}^3$.

steps.title: Calculate the volume
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the rearranged formula:

$$V = \frac{1.50 \times 8.31 \times 423}{202.6}$$

$$V = 26.0 \, \text{dm}^3$$

This calculation earns you the marks for the correct substitution (M1) and the final accurate result (A1).

gdc.instructions: Enter (1.50 * 8.31 * 423) / 202.6 in the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar to find the value.

calculator.description: Calculate the volume by substituting the known values into the rearranged formula.

steps.title: Convert temperatures to Kelvin
steps.content:
In gas law problems, we must always work with absolute temperature in Kelvin ($K$). We convert the Celsius temperatures for our reference (**Source B**) and our target (**Source E**) by adding $273$.

For Source B:
$$T_B = 25 + 273 = 298\text{ K}$$

For Source E:
$$T_E = 150 + 273 = 423\text{ K}$$

steps.title: Identify the proportionality relationship
steps.content:
We use the **Combined Gas Law** relationship derived from the ideal gas equation. This allows us to compare two states of a gas mixture:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{n_2 T_2}$$

Let's compare **Source B** (our reference) to **Source E** (our target):

$$\frac{P_B V_B}{n_B T_B} = \frac{P_E V_E}{n_E T_E}$$

steps.title: Simplify using constant variables
steps.content:
Looking at the table, we notice that both Source B and Source E have a pressure of $202.6\text{ kPa}$. Since $P_B = P_E$, the pressure terms cancel out, simplifying our ratio to:

$$\frac{V_B}{n_B T_B} = \frac{V_E}{n_E T_E}$$

steps.title: Rearrange and solve
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to isolate the unknown volume ($V_E$) and substitute the known values from the table:

$$V_E = V_B \times \left( \frac{n_E}{n_B} \right) \times \left( \frac{T_E}{T_B} \right)$$

$$V_E = 24.5 \times \left( \frac{1.50}{2.00} \right) \times \left( \frac{423}{298} \right)$$

This shows that the volume changes proportionally based on the changes in moles and temperature.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the reference volume by the molar ratio and the temperature ratio.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of the volume and ratios.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the proportional expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume for Source E using the ratio method.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
After performing the calculation, we find:

$$V_E \approx 26.0\text{ dm}^3$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the data provided), the volume of the gas at $150^\circ\text{C}$ is **$26.0\text{ dm}^3$**. 

This method earns you marks for correctly substituting values and identifying the temperature in Kelvin.

parts.content: Suggest why gases in industrial containers are typically stored at high pressures rather than low pressures.
parts.markscheme:
- State that high pressure increases gas density, allowing a larger quantity of gas to be stored in a smaller volume for efficient transport and storage. <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To understand why gases are stored at high pressures, we look at the relationship between pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and the number of moles ($n$) using the **ideal gas equation** from your data booklet. 

$$PV = nRT$$

By rearranging this formula for $n$, we see that for a fixed container volume ($V$) and constant temperature ($T$):

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{PV}{RT} \end{aligned}$$

This shows that the amount of gas ($n$) is directly proportional to the pressure ($P$). Therefore, increasing the pressure increases the **gas density**, meaning more gas particles are packed into the same space.

steps.title: Quantify the Storage Benefit
steps.content:
Let's compare how much gas fits in a $50\text{ dm}^3$ tank at room temperature ($298.15\text{ K}$) at standard pressure ($101.3\text{ kPa}$) versus a typical industrial storage pressure ($20,000\text{ kPa}$).

Using $n = \frac{PV}{RT}$ (where $R = 8.31\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}$):
- At **low pressure** ($101.3\text{ kPa}$): $n \approx 2.04\text{ mol}$
- At **high pressure** ($20,000\text{ kPa}$): $n \approx 403.61\text{ mol}$

Increasing the pressure by roughly 200 times allows us to store nearly **200 times more gas** in the exact same container!

gdc.instructions: Enter (101.3 * 50) / (8.31 * 298.15) for the atmospheric case, then repeat with 20000 for the industrial case.

desmos.instructions: Use the fraction template: \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} and substitute values for both cases.

steps.title: Efficiency and Economical Transport
steps.content:
The primary reason for using **high pressures** is **storage and transport efficiency**. By increasing the gas density:
1. A much larger quantity of gas fits into a smaller, more manageable volume.
2. Fewer containers are required to transport the same mass of gas.
3. It is far more economical and space-efficient for industrial use.
